reason: Sarcasm depends on context, tone, culture, and speaker intent. A wrong prediction may label genuine text as sarcastic or miss subtle sarcasm.
mitigation: Show confidence score, communicate uncertainty, avoid automated punitive use, and require human interpretation.

reason: The model only uses text input and may not have full conversation history, user relationship, timing, or social context.
mitigation: Use optional parent comment context, state that predictions are approximate, and avoid using the model for moderation decisions.

reason: The SARC dataset comes from Reddit and may reflect platform-specific language, communities, humor styles, and demographic patterns.
mitigation: Limit claims to Reddit-style/social-media text and disclose that performance may not generalize to other platforms or cultures.

reason: Social media text may contain personal or sensitive information even if collected publicly.
mitigation: Do not commit raw error samples to GitHub, keep local examples ignored, and use aggregate reporting instead of exposing raw user text.

reason: A high probability score does not mean the prediction is always correct, especially for ambiguous sarcasm.
mitigation: Present confidence as model confidence only, not truth certainty. Include clear wording in the UI.

reason: A hosted app could be misunderstood as a production-ready moderation tool.
mitigation: Add a visible research-demo warning and disable heavy training/execution features on public hosting.

reason: Training used a practical sample size due to local compute constraints, while evaluation used the full held-out test set.
mitigation: Report the training setup transparently and keep scripts/configs reproducible in GitHub.

reason: Sarcasm may vary across cultures, languages, dialects, memes, and community norms.
mitigation: Avoid claiming universal sarcasm detection and recommend further testing on other communities or languages.
